Proposed training and development programme
I plan to pursue an MSc in Social Research Methods at University College London (UCL) and work concurrently as
a Research Assistant at QMUL. I believe that concurrently being a researcher and a student is the most practical
approach, allowing me to continue learning while actively applying my skills in meaningful research endeavours.
These dual roles will complement each other, providing me with transferable skills to excel in both capacities under
expert supervision.
The MSc Social Research Methods is a broad master’s level training programme. It will provide high quality
instruction in the twin pillars of mixed methodology research, quantitative and qualitative methods. This MSc is
designed to develop researchers and provide them with relevant methodological theory and experience.

 Ethnography [term 2]

Dr Smith is a mixed methods researcher with expertise in neuropsychology, health psychology, and behavioural
science. Her research topics include comprehensive care for Parkinson’s Disease, cognitive problems in multiple
sclerosis, and psychological aspects of vestibular disorders.

Noyce is Co-Principal Investigator of the PREDICT-PD study, Principal Investigator for the ELPD project, and
Chief Investigator for the Access-PD study. He is a member of the International Parkinson’s and Movement
Disorder Society (MDS). His roles in the MDS are chair of the epidemiology study group, member of the early
onset PD study, member of the equality of access to care committee, and faculty for the LEAP leadership course.
He is on the steering committee of the Global Parkinson’s Genetics Program (GP2), and he leads training and
networking, and the prodromal work packages.

h has been shaped by my previous experience and learnings. Working on the East London

Employment
Bilingual Research Assistant
From November 2019, I worked as a Bilingual Research Assistant at Queen Mary University of London (QMUL) on
the East London Parkinson’s Disease (ELPD) project, funded by the Michael J. Fox Foundation. The project, led
by Professor Alastair Noyce, is a platform study for under-represented patients with Parkinson’s disease in East
London.
I created a register of 500 Parkinson’s disease patients at The Royal London Hospital. Creation of the register
involved data management and analysis. This involved data cleaning, fixing inconsistencies, secure data storage,
and preparing data for data archiving. I followed Open Science principles and practices to enhance reproducibility.
I recruited approximately 200 new participants to the study. I handled various research tasks, including qualitative
and quantitative data collection, statistical data analysis including linear and non-linear regression models, report
writing, and project management duties. I worked closely with community members to co-develop culturally
appropriate research proto
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Applicant Research Background

Clinical Coordinator
From 2021, I worked as a Clinical Coordinator at Barts Health NHS Trust. My role was funded by Parkinson’s UK.
A significant component of my role was to continue research on the ELPD project.
As a lead applicant, I applied for and was awarded £500 (2021) and £700 (2022) grant to raise Parkinson’s
disease awareness in Tower Hamlets. I engaged over 2,000 people through engagement activities, such as the
Festival of Communities at QMUL and visits to local mosques and parks. I developed educational materials for
patients and their caregivers, conducted quarterly educational sessions, and organised PPIE. The primary focus of
the PPIE was to foster collaboration between researchers and participants, ensuring that the research aligns with
the need and priorities of those directly impacted by it.
Through my clinical coordinator role, I have gained insights into care provision for Parkinson’s disease patients.
For example, diagnostic and treatment pathways, what routine care looks like, and patient and healthcare provider
experiences.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)

Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

h has been shaped by my previous experience and learnings. Working on the East London
Parkinson’s Disease (ELPD) project, fostered a desire to continuing working on Parkinson’s disease and exploring
the impact on the lives of people living with the condition. My work on the ‘Mapping Practices of Participation’
project and with under-served communities has informed the participatory approach I plan to take. The NIHR’s
strategic focus on patient benefit, involvement, and inclusivity underlines my proposed research.
I plan to research the treatment priorities of under-served communities living with Parkinson’s disease in East
London. This will be multi-staged mixed-methods research following James Lind Alliance (JLA) Priority Settings
Partnership (PSP) methods.
The NIHR funds the infrastructure of the JLA to oversee the processes for PSPs. PSPs aim to help patients,
carers and clinicians work together to agree which are the most important evidence uncertainties affecting their
particular interest, in order to influence the prioritisation of future research in that area
[https://www.jla.nihr.ac.uk/about-the-james-lind-alliance/about-psps.htm].

es.
Stage 5: Developing and administering a second survey, asking stakeholders to rank the list of summary

Stage 6: Quantitatively analyse responses to produce a top-ranked list. Host a workshop with diverse stakeholders
to finalize the 'Top 10' priorities using consensus methods, co-facilitated by a PPIE expert from Centre for Public
Engagement, QMUL.
The proposed research has a data validation stage, where the results of the scoping review validate the initial
survey data. An exploratory sequential mixed method stage, where the findings of the qualitative stages determine
second survey, quantitative data collection, and subsequent quantitative data analysis. An explanatory sequential
mixed method stage, where the findings of the quantitative stage are refined by the stakeholder workshop, a
qualitative research method. My proposed research will provide a framework for high quality research experience
of a mixed methods study.
During my subsequent PhD, I will continue to publish and promote the ‘Top 10’ list of priorities and conduct
research focusing on addressing the questions highlighted by stakeholders.

Employment
Bilingual Research Assistant
From November 2019, I worked as a Bilingual Research Assistant at Queen Mary University of London (QMUL) on
the East London Parkinson’s Disease (ELPD) project, funded by the Michael J. Fox Foundation. The project, led
by Professor Alastair Noyce, is a platform study for under-represented patients with Parkinson’s disease in East
London.
I created a register of 500 Parkinson’s disease patients at The Royal London Hospital. Creation of the register
involved data management and analysis. This involved data cleaning, fixing inconsistencies, secure data storage,
and preparing data for data archiving. I followed Open Science principles and practices to enhance reproducibility.
I recruited approximately 200 new participants to the study. I handled various research tasks, including qualitative
and quantitative data collection, statistical data analysis including linear and non-linear regression models, report
writing, and project management duties. I worked closely with community members to co-develop culturally
appropriate research proto
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Applicant Research Background

ruary 2023, I have been working as a Community Engagement Research Assistant on the Mapping
Practices of Participation project, led by Dr Megan Clinch at QMUL and funded by Research England. The project
explores participation through evaluating co-production and Patient Public Involvement and Engagement (PPIE)
activities across the Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry at QMUL.
I have conducted a literature review and thematically analysed content from 43 studies using Braun and Clarke’s
method. I have also drafted interview questions for academics, community organisations, and members of the
public. I have conducted 20 semi-structured interviews, and am using NVivo software to sort, code, and organise
the data.
Fellowship Suitability
My years of experience in academic and community settings, positions me as an excellent candidate for the NIHR
Pre-doctoral Fellowship. Through projects like the ELPD study, I have contributed to the local community in East
London, while 'Mapping Practices of Participation' offers the promise of influencing future participation practices.
These experiences along with my undergraduate dissertation have provided me with a solid foundation in

evidence.text:
Proposed training and development programme
I plan to pursue an MSc in Social Research Methods at University College London (UCL) and work concurrently as
a Research Assistant at QMUL. I believe that concurrently being a researcher and a student is the most practical
approach, allowing me to continue learning while actively applying my skills in meaningful research endeavours.
These dual roles will complement each other, providing me with transferable skills to excel in both capacities under
expert supervision.
The MSc Social Research Methods is a broad master’s level training programme. It will provide high quality
instruction in the twin pillars of mixed methodology research, quantitative and qualitative methods. This MSc is
designed to develop researchers and provide them with relevant methodological theory and experience.
Year 1
 Introduction to Qualitative Methods [term 1]
 Introduction to Regression Analysis [term 1]
 Survey Design [term 1]
 Advanced Qualitative Methods [term 2]
 Advanced Quantitative Methods [term 2]
 Ethnography [term 2]
Year 2
 Systematic Review Design and Planning [term 1]
 Research Engagement, Participation, and Impact [term 1]

ertation [60 credits]
Additional Training
Mixed Methods Course
 Mixed Methods in Health Research, University of Oxford [7 weeks online, 1 week in-person]
This is a comprehensive course focusing on the practical ways to combine qualitative and quantitative
methodologies. I will learn how to design, implement, and analyse mixed methods studies within health and social
care research contexts. This programme provides essential preparation for my fellowship and subsequent PhD.
Patient Public Involvement & Engagement
I have chosen an expansive course due to the participatory nature of my proposed research.
 Participatory Approaches in Public Health Specialisation, Imperial College London [60 hours, online]
 PPIE training offered by the NIHR
 Bespoke PPIE training offered by Centre for Public Engagement, QMUL
Academic & Research Skills
I will undertake a comprehensive programme of short courses and bespoke support offered by the Centre for
Academic and Professional Development, and Joint Research Management Office, QMUL:

evidence.text:
inson’s. However, these priorities have been heavily influenced by
white middle-class groups, with limited responses from ethnic minorities. Since ethnicity is known to influence
epidemiology, clinical manifestation, and the clinical pathway of Parkinson’s disease, my research will aim to
capture the voices of ethnically diverse groups in relation to Parkinson’s disease research priorities.
The research includes:
Stage 1: Forming a steering group with diverse stakeholders to define the priority setting scope, develop a study
protocol, and oversee the process. Potential steering group members have already been identified through the
ELPD study.
Stage 2: Scoping review of existing literature. Developing a survey based on steering group feedback.
Administering this to various stakeholders through multiple channels, seeking input on research questions related
to Parkinson's and diverse ethnic communities.
Stage 3: Collate and qualitatively analyse survey responses using thematic analysis. Group recurring themes into
a long list of summary questions, involving a team of researchers.
Stage 4: Verify summary questions against the literature review to ensure they represent true uncertainti
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
es.
Stage 5: Developing and administering a second survey, asking stakeholders to rank the list of summary
questions.
Stage 6: Quantitatively analyse responses to produce a top-ranked list. Host a workshop with diverse stakeholders
to finalize the 'Top 10' priorities using consensus methods, co-facilitated by a PPIE expert from Centre for Public
Engagement, QMUL.
The proposed research has a data validation stage, where the results of the scoping review validate the initial
survey data. An exploratory sequential mixed method stage, where the findings of the qualitative stages determine
second survey, quantitative data collection, and subsequent quantitative data analysis. An explanatory sequential
mixed method stage, where the findings of the quantitative stage are refined by the stakeholder workshop, a
qualitative research method. My proposed research will provide a framework for high quality research experience
of a mixed methods study.
During my subsequent PhD, I will continue to publish and promote the ‘Top 10’ list of priorities and conduct
research focusing on addressing the questions highlighted by stakeholders.

Parkinson’s Disease (ELPD) project, fostered a desire to continuing working on Parkinson’s disease and exploring
the impact on the lives of people living with the condition. My work on the ‘Mapping Practices of Participation’
project and with under-served communities has informed the participatory approach I plan to take. The NIHR’s
strategic focus on patient benefit, involvement, and inclusivity underlines my proposed research.
I plan to research the treatment priorities of under-served communities living with Parkinson’s disease in East
London. This will be multi-staged mixed-methods research following James Lind Alliance (JLA) Priority Settings
Partnership (PSP) methods.
The NIHR funds the infrastructure of the JLA to oversee the processes for PSPs. PSPs aim to help patients,
carers and clinicians work together to agree which are the most important evidence uncertainties affecting their
particular interest, in order to influence the prioritisation of future research in that area
[https://www.jla.nihr.ac.uk/about-the-james-lind-alliance/about-psps.htm].
PSPs have previously been outlined in Park

Overall Expected Outcomes

evidence.text:
es.
Stage 5: Developing and administering a second survey, asking stakeholders to rank the list of summary
questions.
Stage 6: Quantitatively analyse responses to produce a top-ranked list. Host a workshop with diverse stakeholders
to finalize the 'Top 10' priorities using consensus methods, co-facilitated by a PPIE expert from Centre for Public
Engagement, QMUL.
The proposed research has a data validation stage, where the results of the scoping review validate the initial
survey data. An exploratory sequential mixed method stage, where the findings of the qualitative stages determine
second survey, quantitative data collection, and subsequent quantitative data analysis. An explanatory sequential
mixed method stage, where the findings of the quantitative stage are refined by the stakeholder workshop, a
qualitative research method. My proposed research will provide a framework for high quality research experience
of a mixed methods study.
During my subsequent PhD, I will continue to publish and promote the ‘Top 10’ list of priorities and conduct
research focusing on addressing the questions highlighted by stakeholders.
